risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The data manager used computer-generated randomization to allocate the 738 participants to either the intervention or nonintervention group." Comment: Computer-generated randomization is a truly random allocation method, so the sequence generation is adequate and at low risk of bias.

Quote: "The data manager used computer-generated randomization to allocate the 738 participants to either the intervention or nonintervention group" Comment: This reports sequence generation but gives no explicit information about how allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is described, so foreknowledge cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The PWE and an identified designated supporter (caregiver) were invited to a one-day education program on epilepsy..." and "The nonintervention group received the health education after the second assessment." Comment: Participants and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded; the intervention was obvious and involved direct educational workshops, so knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect subjective outcomes such as self-reported adherence, seizure reporting, and beliefs/attitudes.

Quote: "The laboratory technicians conducting the assays were blinded to the randomization. The questionnaires were administered by trained field staff, both at baseline and follow-up." Comment: Outcome assessment was partly blinded for the laboratory-based plasma AED assays, but blinding of the field staff administering questionnaires and assessing self-reported adherence, seizure frequency, and KEBAS was not reported. Because the study used a mix of objective lab measurements and subjective/self-reported outcomes, and the paper does not clearly state whether the questionnaire assessors were blinded, detection bias for outcome assessment remains unclear.

Quote: "Data were analyzed for 581 PWE observed at both time points. ... In the 157 PWE not seen after 1 year (because of death [45.2%], outmigration [48.4%], and withdrawal [5.7%] [Fig.1]), there were no statistically significant differences in demographic and epilepsy-related characteristics between the groups." Comment: Attrition was about 21% overall (157/738), but the paper reports that reasons were mainly death and outmigration, and that those lost did not differ significantly between randomized groups. This suggests no clear differential attrition by arm; although some outcomes had missing blood samples, the available information does not indicate outcome-related imbalance sufficient to bias the main comparison.
